This flag is crucial for managing the transition between different types of input blocks and ensuring the correct processing and error handling.

Acts as a data register to store the difference between two timestamps. This value is used to adjust the timestamp in the next cycle, ensuring accurate time synchronization. It influences the logic by providing an incremental update to the timestamp, which is crucial for maintaining the correct time in the system. The register is updated at each clock cycle with the difference between the current and previous timestamps, allowing for precise adjustments based on the time elapsed.

Acts as a data register to store and process incoming data based on control information. The data is either passed through, masked, or shifted according to specific conditions. This register is crucial for handling different data formats and ensuring proper data flow. It influences the state machine's behavior by providing processed data that determines the next state and output signals. The data register also plays a role in error detection and timestamp adjustments, contributing to the overall functionality of the system.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the presence of a frame check sequence (FCS) error. This flag is set when the CRC validation fails during the termination of a packet, signaling that the packet's integrity has been compromised. The flag is used to update the output control signals and is reset at the start of a new packet or when the system is reset.

Acts as a data register to store and update the user metadata associated with the outgoing data packet. It is used to carry additional information, such as error flags and timestamp data, alongside the main data payload. The register is updated in various states of the FSM, particularly during the payload and last states, where it may include CRC validity and error conditions. This ensures that the receiving end can interpret the packet's integrity and timing accurately.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the validity of data being transferred. It is asserted during the payload and last states to signify that the data is ready for processing and can be sent out. This control signal ensures that the data transfer occurs only when there is valid data, thereby synchronizing the data flow and preventing invalid or incomplete data from being processed.

Acts as a reset signal to initialize the state machine and associated registers. When asserted, it sets the state to an idle condition, clears various control flags, and resets data registers. This ensures that the system starts from a known, consistent state after a reset event.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the type of input block in the next cycle. It is used to determine the next state and control the flow of data and control signals. This flag helps in identifying whether the current block is a start, data, or termination block, which influences the FSM's behavior, such as initiating packet processing, managing data validity, and handling packet termination. The flag also assists in error detection and lane swapping, ensuring that the data is processed correctly and efficiently.

Acts as a data register to store and update timestamp values. It is used to manage the rollover of nanoseconds and adjust the timestamp based on specific conditions. The register is updated when a start packet is detected, and it stores the current timestamp value for later use. This ensures that the timestamp is correctly maintained and synchronized with the incoming data, allowing for accurate time-stamping of events.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the presence of a specific type of error in the frame check sequence (FCS). This flag is updated during the processing of incoming data and control signals, and it is set when an FCS error is detected. The flag helps in maintaining the integrity of the data by signaling such errors, which can be used for further error handling or logging.

Serves as a state flag to indicate the type of input block being processed. It influences the state transitions and data handling within the FSM. When the flag indicates a start condition, it triggers the transition from the idle state to the payload processing state. During payload processing, it controls how data is managed, including setting control flags and managing data validity. It also handles packet termination by adjusting data keep signals and checking for CRC validity. If an error is detected, it sets error flags and resets the FSM to the idle state.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data packet. It is set during the final cycle of packet processing, signaling the completion of the current packet and preparing the system for the next one. This flag is crucial for managing the flow of data and ensuring that the receiving end correctly identifies the end of each packet.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the presence of an invalid or erroneous block in the received data. It is set to 1 when the system detects an unexpected or unsupported block type, signaling an error condition. This flag helps in identifying and handling errors in the data stream, ensuring that the system can take appropriate corrective actions, such as marking the input type as an error and propagating this information to subsequent processing stages.

module_description: This code implements a combinational logic block for generating a cyclic redundancy check (CRC) value. It processes input data and a state register to update the CRC, ensuring data integrity. The updated CRC value and the next state are output, which then updates the state register for the next cycle. This block is crucial for error detection in data transmission or storage, verifying that the data remains error-free throughout its processing.

block_descriptions.description: This code implements a combinational logic block that processes an 8-byte data word. It iterates over each byte, checking a corresponding control flag. If the flag is set, the byte is passed through unchanged; otherwise, it is replaced with a zero value. This operation effectively masks out bytes based on the control flags, preparing the data for further processing, such as FCS (Frame Check Sequence) calculation.

This code implements a combinational logic block that processes data based on the value of a control signal. Depending on the value of the control signal, it generates specific output data and types. The output data is formed by combining parts of a state register and input data, with some bits inverted. The output type is set to indicate different states such as data, termination, or idle. Additionally, an offset value is set based on the control signal, which likely influences further processing or timing.

This code implements a finite state machine (FSM) for processing and transmitting Ethernet frames. The FSM transitions between states based on input conditions and control flags. In the idle state, it waits for data and initializes frame parameters. When valid data is detected, it transitions to the payload state, where it processes and transmits the frame data, updating CRC and frame counters. If padding is required, it enters a padding state to ensure the frame meets the minimum length. After the payload, it calculates and appends the frame check sequence (FCS). If an error occurs, it handles the error and transitions to an inter-frame gap (IFG) state to send idle characters before returning to the idle state. The FSM also manages lane swapping and deficit idle count for proper frame transmission.

This code implements a synchronous state machine that processes and encodes data for transmission. It updates multiple registers on the rising edge of the clock, including state, control flags, and data registers. The state machine handles different types of data, such as idle, error, start, and termination packets, and encodes them based on the current state. It also manages lane swapping, frame synchronization, and time-stamping. Time-stamp adjustments and validity checks are performed based on specific conditions. The system includes reset functionality to initialize all registers to their default values when a reset signal is active. Additionally, it updates a CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) state based on control signals.

signal_descriptions.description: Acts as a state flag to indicate the presence of an error in the frame. This flag is set when specific error conditions are detected, such as invalid data or user-defined error flags. It influences the state transitions and output type, ensuring that the system can handle and mark frames with errors appropriately. The flag is reset at the beginning of each new frame and is used to determine whether to transition to an error handling state or continue with normal frame processing.

Acts as a data register to store and manage the data being processed for transmission. It holds the current data value and is updated based on the next data value and control conditions. The data register is used in various encoding operations, such as forming start, data, and termination packets, and is influenced by lane swapping and other control signals. This register ensures that the correct data is available for encoding and transmission, and it is reset to a default value during system initialization.

This code implements a combinational logic block that processes an 8-byte data word. It iterates over each byte, checking a corresponding control flag. If the control flag is set, the byte is passed through unchanged; otherwise, it is replaced with a zero value. This operation effectively masks out bytes based on the control flags, preparing the data for further processing, such as FCS (Frame Check Sequence) calculation.

Acts as a data register to store the minimum frame count required for an Ethernet frame. It is initialized in the idle state and decremented during the payload state to ensure the frame meets the minimum length. If the frame does not meet the minimum length, it triggers padding to extend the frame. The value is updated based on the current state and input conditions, ensuring that the transmitted frame adheres to the required specifications.

Acts as a control signal to manage the updating of the CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) state. It is asserted during the payload and padding states to ensure that the CRC is continuously updated as data is processed and transmitted. This control signal helps maintain the integrity of the data by calculating the CRC in real-time, which is later appended to the frame for error detection.

Acts as a state register to store intermediate states of a cyclic redundancy check (CRC) calculation. This state register is updated on the rising edge of the clock and is used to maintain the CRC state across multiple clock cycles. The register is reset to a specific value when a reset signal is active, ensuring that the CRC calculation starts from a known state. During normal operation, the register is updated with new values based on the current state and control signals, which are essential for maintaining the integrity of the data being processed.

Acts as a state flag to track the remaining minimum frame length during data transmission. It is initialized to a predefined value and decremented as data is processed. If the remaining length exceeds a threshold, it continues to decrement; otherwise, it is set to zero. This flag ensures that the frame meets the minimum length requirement before transitioning to the next state, such as padding or appending the frame check sequence.

Acts as a clock signal to synchronize the state machine and data processing. On each rising edge, it triggers the update of multiple registers, including state, control flags, and data. The clock ensures that the state machine transitions correctly and processes different types of data, such as idle, error, start, and termination packets. It also manages lane swapping, frame synchronization, and time-stamping, performing adjustments and validity checks. The clock signal is crucial for maintaining the timing and coordination of the entire system, ensuring that all operations are synchronized and executed in the correct order. Additionally, it enables the reset functionality, initializing all registers to their default values when a reset signal is active.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the number of empty bytes in a data register. This flag influences the generation of output data and types, which are formed by combining parts of a state register and input data. The flag's value determines the specific output data, type, and an offset value, which likely affects further processing or timing. During state transitions, this flag is updated based on the current state and input conditions, ensuring that the system correctly handles frame data, padding, and error conditions.

Acts as a reset signal to initialize the system. When asserted, it sets all state and control registers to their default values, effectively resetting the state machine and associated logic. This ensures that the system starts in a known state, with all data and control paths cleared, ready for new operations.

Serves as a data register to store the difference between two time-stamp values. This difference is calculated and stored during each clock cycle to track the increment in time. It influences the logic by providing a mechanism to adjust and synchronize time-stamp values, ensuring accurate time-stamping for transmitted data. The value is updated based on the current and previous time-stamp, and it is used in various conditions to manage the time-stamp adjustments and validity checks.

Serves as a state flag to manage the time-stamping process. It is updated during the start of a new frame and influences the time-stamp register based on lane swapping and specific enable conditions. When enabled, it triggers the adjustment and validation of the time-stamp data, ensuring accurate synchronization and encoding for transmission. The state flag also interacts with control signals to determine the format and validity of the time-stamp, contributing to the overall integrity and timing of the transmitted data.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the validity of a specific type of data delay. It is used to control the selection and processing of data types in the state machine. When asserted, it triggers the use of a modified data type for further processing. This flag is set based on certain conditions, such as the presence of specific data types and lane swapping, and is reset during the initialization or when not needed. The flag ensures that the correct data type is used for encoding and transmission, maintaining the integrity and correctness of the data flow.

Acts as a data register to store and propagate input data through the state machine. It holds the current data value and is used to form the output data in various states, such as payload, padding, and FCS (Frame Check Sequence) generation. The data register ensures that the correct data is available for processing and transmission, and it is updated based on the incoming data and the current state of the FSM. This register is crucial for maintaining data integrity and synchronization throughout the frame transmission process.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data frame. It influences the FSM by signaling the last data transfer, which triggers the transition to subsequent states for finalizing the frame, such as appending the FCS or handling errors. When this flag is asserted, it also affects the readiness of the system to accept new data, ensuring that the current frame is fully processed before starting a new one.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the type of output data in the final stage of frame transmission. It is set to specific values based on the current state and conditions, such as when the frame is being terminated or when idle characters are being sent. This state flag influences the processing and transmission of the frame by signaling the type of data to be output, which can be termination, idle, or error. The flag ensures that the correct data type is generated and transmitted, maintaining the integrity and format of the Ethernet frame.

Serves as a data register to store the current frame status. This status is used to determine whether the frame is still active or has been completed. It influences the state transitions and data flow, ensuring that the frame is processed correctly and that the system transitions to the appropriate state based on the frame's status. The register is updated during each clock cycle, reflecting the ongoing state of the frame and enabling the system to manage the transmission process effectively.

Acts as a data register to store the most recent time-stamp value. This register is updated with the current time-stamp during each clock cycle, allowing the system to track and compare time-stamp differences. The stored value is used for calculating time-stamp increments and for adjusting the time-stamp data before it is transmitted. This ensures that the time-stamp information is accurate and synchronized with the data being processed.

Acts as a data register to store and format the encoded data for transmission. It holds the processed data that is ready to be sent, based on the current state and control signals. The register is updated with the appropriate data pattern, including idle, error, start, and termination packets, according to the specified output type. This ensures that the data is correctly formatted and synchronized before being transmitted.

Acts as a state flag to control the inter-frame gap (IFG) duration. It is set based on the current state and specific conditions, influencing the timing and length of the IFG. This flag helps in determining the number of idle characters to be sent between frames, ensuring proper frame spacing and adherence to Ethernet standards. The value of this flag is adjusted according to the configuration and current state, which affects the overall frame transmission process.

Acts as a state flag to indicate an underflow condition during data transmission. It is set when the input data is not valid, signaling that the expected data is missing. This flag helps in error handling and ensures that the system can take appropriate actions, such as dropping the frame or transitioning to an error state, to maintain the integrity of the data being transmitted.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the type of data being processed in the current state. It influences the encoding and transmission of different types of packets, such as idle, error, start, and termination. The state flag determines the specific encoding and synchronization patterns for the data, ensuring proper frame structure and error handling. During lane swapping, it also affects how the data is rearranged and encoded, maintaining the integrity of the transmitted frames.

Acts as a state flag to manage the inter-frame gap (IFG) duration. It is updated based on the current state and specific conditions, such as the need for padding or error handling. The state flag ensures that the correct number of idle characters are sent between frames, maintaining the required IFG. It also considers deficit idle count and lane swapping to properly handle frame transmission and synchronization.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the validity of time-stamp data. It is set based on specific conditions and input signals, ensuring that the time-stamp data is only considered valid when appropriate. This control signal helps in synchronizing and validating the time-stamp data during the transmission process, ensuring that only accurate and relevant time-stamp information is used. The signal is reset to an inactive state at the start of each clock cycle and is updated based on the presence of valid time-stamp data and the current configuration settings.

Acts as a data register to store the next value of the output data. This register is updated based on the current state and input conditions, ensuring that the correct data is available for transmission. It holds the data to be sent in the next cycle, which can include payload, preamble, start frame delimiter, or idle characters, depending on the FSM state. The register is crucial for maintaining the flow of data through the system, ensuring that the output is synchronized with the state transitions and control signals.

Acts as a control signal to indicate specific conditions during frame transmission. It is used to detect and handle errors in the payload, influencing the state transitions and error handling within the FSM. When asserted, it triggers an error state, affecting the output type and subsequent state transitions, ensuring proper error management and frame integrity.

Acts as a state flag to indicate an underflow condition. It is updated based on the next state of the underflow signal and is used to track whether there has been a data underflow during the processing. This flag helps in error detection and handling, ensuring that the system can respond appropriately to underflow conditions by potentially triggering corrective actions or signaling the error to higher-level logic.

Acts as a control signal to reset the CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) state. It is asserted at specific points in the state machine, such as when transitioning to the idle state or after completing the frame check sequence (FCS) calculation. This ensures that the CRC state is initialized to a known value, typically all ones, before starting a new frame. The control signal helps maintain the integrity of the CRC computation by resetting it at appropriate times during the frame processing.

Acts as a state flag to manage lane swapping during data transmission. It is used to determine whether the data lanes should be swapped, which affects the output data and type. The flag is set based on the current state of the finite state machine and specific conditions, such as the inter-frame gap count and deficit idle count. When asserted, it triggers the swapping of data lanes, ensuring proper frame alignment and transmission.

Acts as a state flag to manage the inter-frame gap (IFG) and ensure proper frame transmission. It is used to track the number of idle characters that need to be sent when the frame length is shorter than the minimum required. This flag is updated based on the current state and conditions, such as the frame length and lane swapping, to maintain the correct number of idle characters for the next frame.

This signal acts as a data register that stores a portion of the processed data. It holds the upper 32 bits of the next output data, which is used in the construction of the final output data. When lane swapping is enabled, this data is combined with the lower 32 bits to form the complete 64-bit output data. This process ensures that the data is correctly formatted and aligned for transmission, supporting various packet types such as start, data, and termination packets. The signal is part of a larger system that encodes and synchronizes data, managing different states and control flags to handle the data flow efficiently.

Acts as a state flag to manage the inter-frame gap (IFG) and ensure proper frame transmission. It is updated based on the current state and input conditions, such as the inter-frame gap count and lane swapping. This flag helps in determining the number of idle characters to send between frames, ensuring that the IFG meets the required length. If the IFG count is insufficient, it triggers additional idle characters to be sent, maintaining the correct spacing between frames.

The control signal is used to manage the inter-frame gap (IFG) duration, which is the idle period between transmitted frames. It determines the number of idle characters to be sent based on the configured IFG value and other conditions like lane swapping and deficit idle count. This ensures that the transmission adheres to Ethernet standards by maintaining the correct spacing between frames, preventing frame collisions and ensuring proper synchronization. The control signal is updated in the FCS and IFG states to reflect the remaining idle characters to be transmitted, and it influences the state transitions, particularly when transitioning back to the idle state.

This signal acts as a data register that holds the processed data before it is transmitted. It is used to store the 8-byte data word after applying a mask based on control flags, which effectively zeroes out certain bytes. This processed data is then used in the payload and padding states of the finite state machine (FSM) to ensure the frame meets the required format and length. The FSM uses this data register to manage the transmission of valid data, handle padding, and prepare for the frame check sequence (FCS) calculation.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the continuation of the current frame. It is used to manage the flow of data and control the transition between different states in the FSM. The flag is updated based on the validity and last status of the input data, ensuring that the frame processing continues until the end of the frame is detected. This flag also influences the readiness of the system to accept new data, thereby controlling the overall data flow and state transitions within the FSM.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the presence of an error in the frame. It is set during the payload processing if certain error conditions are met, such as invalid data or user-defined error flags. This flag influences the state transitions, particularly moving the FSM to an error handling state where it terminates the packet with an error and sends an inter-frame gap before returning to the idle state. The flag ensures that any detected errors are properly handled and signaled, maintaining the integrity of the transmission process.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the type of data being encoded for transmission. It determines the synchronization and control signals for different packet types, such as idle, error, start, and termination packets. This flag is updated based on the current state and influences the encoding process, ensuring that the correct header and data are generated for each packet type. During reset, it initializes to a default value, ensuring the system starts in a known state.

The signal acts as a control flag to mask and process data bytes. It is used to determine whether each byte in an 8-byte data word should be passed through or replaced with a zero value. This operation ensures that only the relevant bytes are considered for further processing, such as FCS calculation. The control flag effectively filters out unwanted data, preparing the payload for transmission. During the FSM's payload state, this flag helps manage the data flow and update CRC and frame counters, ensuring that the transmitted data is accurate and meets the required conditions.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the readiness of the input data for processing. It is updated on the rising edge of the clock and is set based on the next state of the signal. This control signal is part of a larger state machine that manages data flow, lane swapping, and frame synchronization. The signal ensures that data is only processed when the system is ready, thus synchronizing the data transfer and preventing data loss or corruption. During a reset, this control signal is initialized to a low state, indicating that the system is not ready to accept new data.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the presence of valid data. It triggers the transition from the idle state to the payload state, enabling the processing and transmission of frame data. When asserted, it also influences the updating of CRC and frame counters, and it is used to manage the flow of data through different states, including padding and error handling. The signal ensures that data is only processed when it is valid, maintaining synchronization and proper frame transmission.

Acts as a state flag to manage the inter-frame gap (IFG) duration. It is used to count the number of idle characters that need to be sent between frames to ensure proper frame spacing. The value is updated based on the configuration and current state, and it influences the transition to the next state, such as moving from the IFG state back to the idle state once the required number of idle characters has been transmitted.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the validity of a time-stamp in a specific format. It is set to zero at the start of each clock cycle and updated based on the presence of a valid time-stamp tag. This flag helps in synchronizing the time-stamp data with the control signals, ensuring that the time-stamp is only considered valid under certain conditions, such as when the time-stamp format is Time-of-Day (TOD) and the control signal indicates a valid time-stamp. The flag is used to trigger the update of the time-stamp register and to propagate the validity status to other parts of the system.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the readiness of the receiver to accept data. It is asserted when the receiver is ready to process incoming data, enabling the sender to transfer data. This signal is used to synchronize data flow and manage the state transitions in the finite state machine, ensuring that data is only transferred when the receiver is prepared to handle it. The signal is de-asserted when the receiver is not ready, preventing data loss or corruption.

Acts as a control signal to manage the readiness of the input data for processing. It is asserted to indicate that the system is ready to accept new data, and it is de-asserted when the system is not ready. This control signal synchronizes the data flow, ensuring that data is only processed when the system is in the correct state and conditions are met. It plays a crucial role in the state transitions, enabling the FSM to handle different stages of frame processing, such as payload transfer, padding, and inter-frame gap management.

Acts as a control signal to initiate the transmission of an Ethernet frame. It is set when valid data is detected and the system is enabled, triggering the start of the frame with preamble and start frame delimiter (SFD). This signal is crucial for transitioning the state machine from the idle state to the payload state, where the actual frame data is processed and transmitted.

Serves as a data register to store and output specific frame check sequence (FCS) information. Depending on the state of the control signal, it holds parts of the CRC state and input data, with some bits inverted. This data is used in the final stages of frame transmission to ensure the integrity of the transmitted data. The content of this register is critical for generating the correct FCS, which is appended to the end of the Ethernet frame.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the start of a new frame. When asserted, it triggers the initialization of packet transmission, including setting up time-stamp values and determining the type of start packet. It also influences the handling of lane swapping and the encoding of data for transmission. The flag is reset at the beginning of each new frame and is used to synchronize the start of data processing and transmission in the system.

Acts as a state flag to control lane swapping during data transmission. It influences the arrangement of data lanes, ensuring proper frame transmission and inter-frame gap management. When asserted, it triggers the swapping of data lanes, which is crucial for maintaining the correct order of data bits. This flag is updated based on the current state and specific conditions, such as the inter-frame gap count and deficit idle count, to ensure that the data is transmitted correctly and efficiently.

Acts as a state flag to determine the current operational mode of the finite state machine. It dictates the transition between different states such as idle, payload processing, padding, and inter-frame gap. The state flag influences the flow of data, control signals, and error handling, ensuring that each step in the frame transmission process is executed correctly. Based on the current state, it enables or disables specific operations, updates control flags, and manages the encoding and transmission of data.

This signal acts as a data register that holds an 8-byte word. The combinational logic block processes each byte, using a control flag to determine whether to pass the byte through or replace it with zero. This masking operation prepares the data for subsequent processing, such as FCS calculation, by ensuring only the relevant bytes are considered.

module_description: This module implements a synchronous state machine designed for data encoding and transmission. It processes various types of data, including idle, error, start, and termination packets, and encodes them based on the current state. The state machine manages lane swapping, frame synchronization, and time-stamping, ensuring accurate and reliable data transmission. It also includes functionality to update a CRC state for error detection. The system is clocked and can be reset to initialize all registers, ensuring proper operation and handling of different data types.

block_descriptions.description: This code implements a state machine for processing input data and generating corresponding output codes. It operates in different states, such as idle, segment processing, and final zero appending. In the idle state, it waits for valid input data and decides whether to store a zero code or initiate a new segment. During segment processing, it counts the number of non-zero bytes and stores them, updating the count and checking for the end of the segment. If a zero or error is detected, it transitions to a final zero state, where it appends a zero code and closes the frame. In zero framing mode, it appends an additional zero code to terminate the frame. The state machine ensures proper handling of input data, segment counting, and frame termination.

This code implements a state machine with two states: an idle state and a segment output state. In the idle state, it waits for a valid condition to transfer a code byte and load a counter. If the code byte meets certain conditions, it remains in the idle state; otherwise, it transitions to the segment output state. In the segment output state, it transfers data bytes and decrements the counter. When the counter reaches one, it returns to the idle state to fetch another code byte. The state machine controls data and control signals, ensuring proper data flow and synchronization.

This code implements a synchronous state machine with reset functionality. It updates the state and count registers for both input and output on each clock edge. The state registers transition to their next states, and the count registers are updated accordingly. If the reset signal is active, the input and output state registers are set to their idle states, effectively resetting the system. This logic is used to manage state transitions and count values in a controlled manner, ensuring that the system can be reset to a known initial state.

This code implements a combinational logic block for data transfer control. It manages the flow of data from an input to an output, using intermediate storage when necessary. The block checks if the input is ready and decides whether to transfer the data directly to the output or store it temporarily. If the input is not ready but the output is, it moves the temporarily stored data to the output. Control flags determine the data transfer paths, ensuring that data is only moved when both the source and destination are in the correct state.

This code implements a synchronous data path control with reset functionality. On each clock edge, it updates several control and data registers based on the current state and control signals. The main function is to manage the flow of data from internal and temporary storage to an output interface. Depending on the control flags, it either stores new data from the internal source to the output or to a temporary buffer. If a reset signal is active, all relevant control and data registers are cleared. This ensures that the data path is properly synchronized and controlled, maintaining the integrity of the data flow.

block_descriptions.code:
code_fifo_inst (
    .clk(clk),
    .rst(rst),
    // AXI input
    .s_axis_tdata(code_fifo_in_tdata),
    .s_axis_tkeep(0),
    .s_axis_tvalid(code_fifo_in_tvalid),
    .s_axis_tready(code_fifo_in_tready),
    .s_axis_tlast(code_fifo_in_tlast),
    .s_axis_tid(0),
    .s_axis_tdest(0),
    .s_axis_tuser(code_fifo_in_tuser),
    // AXI output
    .m_axis_tdata(code_fifo_out_tdata),
    .m_axis_tkeep(),
    .m_axis_tvalid(code_fifo_out_tvalid),
    .m_axis_tready(code_fifo_out_tready),
    .m_axis_tlast(code_fifo_out_tlast),
    .m_axis_tid(),
    .m_axis_tdest(),
    .m_axis_tuser(code_fifo_out_tuser),
    // Status
    .status_overflow(),
    .status_bad_frame(),
    .status_good_frame()
);

axis_fifo #(
    .DEPTH(256),
    .DATA_WIDTH(8),
    .KEEP_ENABLE(0),
    .LAST_ENABLE(1),
    .ID_ENABLE(0),
    .DEST_ENABLE(0),
    .USER_ENABLE(0),
    .FRAME_FIFO(0)
)
block_descriptions.description: This code implements an AXI stream FIFO (First-In-First-Out) buffer. It synchronizes and buffers data between an input and output AXI stream interface. The FIFO handles data, user-defined signals, and end-of-frame indicators. It operates on a clock signal and can be reset. The buffer has a fixed depth and width, and it supports flow control through valid and ready signals for both input and output. Additionally, it provides status signals to indicate overflow, bad frame, and good frame conditions.

block_descriptions.code:
data_fifo_inst (
    .clk(clk),
    .rst(rst),
    // AXI input
    .s_axis_tdata(data_fifo_in_tdata),
    .s_axis_tkeep(0),
    .s_axis_tvalid(data_fifo_in_tvalid),
    .s_axis_tready(data_fifo_in_tready),
    .s_axis_tlast(data_fifo_in_tlast),
    .s_axis_tid(0),
    .s_axis_tdest(0),
    .s_axis_tuser(0),
    // AXI output
    .m_axis_tdata(data_fifo_out_tdata),
    .m_axis_tkeep(),
    .m_axis_tvalid(data_fifo_out_tvalid),
    .m_axis_tready(data_fifo_out_tready),
    .m_axis_tlast(data_fifo_out_tlast),
    .m_axis_tid(),
    .m_axis_tdest(),
    .m_axis_tuser(),
    // Status
    .status_overflow(),
    .status_bad_frame(),
    .status_good_frame()
);
block_descriptions.description: This code instantiates a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) buffer for data handling. It interfaces with AXI (Advanced eXtensible Interface) for both input and output. The FIFO operates synchronously, using a clock and reset signal. It receives data from an input source, including data, valid, ready, and last signals, and outputs the data to a destination with similar control signals. The FIFO also provides status signals to indicate overflow, bad frame, and good frame conditions, allowing for monitoring and error detection.

signal_descriptions.description: Acts as a data register to hold input values for processing. It influences the state machine by providing the current byte of data to be evaluated and stored. The data register is used to determine whether to store a zero code, initiate a new segment, or continue counting non-zero bytes. During the final stages, it helps in appending necessary codes and closing the frame. The state machine uses this data to transition between different states and manage the encoding process effectively.

Acts as a state flag to control the flow of data processing and frame management. It determines the current operational mode, such as waiting for input, processing segments, or appending final codes. The state flag transitions based on the presence of valid input data, the type of data (zero or non-zero), and the end of segment conditions. It ensures that the system correctly handles different states, including idle, segment processing, and frame termination, and manages the transition between these states to maintain proper data flow and frame integrity.

Acts as a control signal to manage the validity of data being transferred. It is asserted when the input is ready and the output can accept new data, enabling the transfer from an internal register to the output. If the output is not ready, the signal facilitates storing the data in a temporary location. When the output becomes ready, the temporarily stored data is then moved to the output. This control signal ensures that data is only transferred when both the source and destination are in the correct state, maintaining proper synchronization and flow.

Acts as a control signal to manage data transfer between internal and output interfaces. It determines whether the data should be transferred directly to the output or stored in a temporary buffer. When asserted, it enables the transfer of data from the internal source to the output, ensuring that the data flow is synchronized and controlled. If the output is not ready, the control signal facilitates storing the data temporarily until the output is ready to accept it. This ensures that data is only moved when both the source and destination are in the correct state, maintaining the integrity of the data flow.

Acts as a control signal to manage the flow of data into a FIFO buffer. It indicates when the buffer is ready to accept new data, enabling the state machine to push data and associated control information into the FIFO. This signal is crucial for synchronizing the data transfer and ensuring that the buffer does not overflow. When asserted, it allows the state machine to transition between different states, such as processing segments or appending final codes, based on the current input data and frame conditions.

Acts as a control signal to manage the readiness of the data path for transferring data. It is updated on each clock edge and, along with other control signals, determines whether data from internal or temporary storage should be moved to the output. This signal ensures that the data transfer is synchronized and controlled, maintaining the integrity of the data flow. During a reset, it is cleared to ensure the system is in a known state.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data segment. It is used to synchronize the transfer of the last data byte in a segment, ensuring that the state machine transitions correctly between states. When this flag is asserted, it signals the completion of the current data segment and triggers the state machine to prepare for the next code or data segment. This synchronization is crucial for maintaining the integrity of the data flow and ensuring that each segment is processed completely before moving on to the next.

Acts as a data register to store and transfer encoded data within the state machine. It holds the value of the code to be written into the FIFO, which is used to manage the encoding process. The data register is updated based on the current state and input conditions, such as when a zero or non-zero byte is detected. This ensures that the correct code is stored and propagated through the system, facilitating the proper encoding and framing of the input data.

Acts as a control signal to manage data transfer between an input and an output. When the output is ready, it enables the direct transfer of data from the input. If the output is not ready, it directs the data to temporary storage. When the input is not ready but the output is, it transfers the data from the temporary storage to the output. This ensures that data is only moved when both the source and destination are in the correct state, preventing data loss or corruption.

Acts as a state flag to control the transition between different operational states in a state machine. It determines whether the system remains in an idle state, waiting for valid conditions to transfer data, or moves to a segment output state to process and transfer data bytes. The state flag ensures that the system transitions appropriately based on the completion of data transfers and the status of the counter, thus managing the flow and synchronization of data and control signals.

Acts as a data register to store the count of remaining data bytes to be transferred. This count is initialized when a code byte is loaded and decremented as each data byte is sent. The register influences the state transitions by determining when all data bytes have been transferred, triggering a return to the idle state to fetch the next code byte. It ensures that the correct number of data bytes are processed in each segment, maintaining the integrity of the data flow.

Acts as a control signal to manage the transfer of data from temporary storage to the output. It is asserted when the input is not ready but the output is, allowing the temporarily stored data to be moved to the output. This control signal ensures that data is only transferred when the output is ready and the input is not, thus maintaining the correct flow and synchronization of data.

Acts as a state flag to indicate special conditions in the data being transferred. It is set when specific conditions are met, such as the last byte of a code segment or a particular code value, and it influences the state machine's transition and data handling. This flag ensures that the system correctly processes and marks significant events in the data stream, maintaining the integrity and synchronization of the data flow.

Acts as a data register to hold intermediate data values during the state transitions of a state machine. This register is updated with new data when specific conditions are met, such as valid input and ready signals. It stores code or data bytes that are then transferred to the output interface. The register ensures that the data is correctly synchronized and available for further processing, maintaining the integrity of the data flow in the system.

Acts as a control signal to manage the readiness of the input data interface. It is used to enable or disable the acceptance of new data based on the current state of the state machine. When asserted, it allows the input data to be processed and stored, while deassertion prevents new data from being accepted. This control signal ensures that data processing only occurs under valid conditions and helps in managing the flow of data through different states of the state machine.

Acts as a clock signal to synchronize the operation of the system. It triggers the update of state and count registers, manages data flow between internal and temporary storage, and controls the operation of FIFO buffers. On each rising edge, it ensures that the system's state and data are updated in a controlled manner. If a reset signal is active, it resets the system to a known initial state, ensuring proper synchronization and data integrity.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the validity of data being written into a FIFO. It is asserted when there is valid input data to be stored, and it synchronizes the data transfer between the input source and the FIFO. This control signal ensures that data is only written to the FIFO when it is ready to accept new data, thus preventing data loss or corruption. The signal is managed by a state machine that processes the input data and decides when to store it based on the current state and input conditions.

Acts as a user-defined signal to propagate error or status information. It is used to indicate whether an error has occurred during the processing of the input data. This signal is set when an error is detected, and it influences the generation of the final output code. In the final state, it determines whether the frame should be marked as failed, which is then propagated to the output. This ensures that any errors in the input data are correctly reflected in the output, maintaining the integrity of the data stream.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data frame. When asserted, it triggers the closing of the current frame and initiates the transition to the next state. It is used to manage the flow of data and ensure that the frame is properly terminated, especially in cases where a zero or error is detected. This flag also helps in synchronizing the state machine transitions and ensuring that the correct codes are appended to the frame before returning to an idle state.

Acts as a state flag to manage the flow of data from an internal source to an output or temporary storage. It is used to indicate whether data should be transferred to the output directly or stored temporarily. The flag is updated based on the readiness of the input and output, ensuring that data is only moved when both the source and destination are in the correct state. This helps in synchronizing the data transfer process and maintaining the integrity of the data path.

Acts as a state flag to control the transition between different states in a state machine. It influences the logic by determining whether the state machine should remain in the current state or move to the next state based on specific conditions. When asserted, it indicates that a particular condition has been met, such as the end of a data segment or the presence of a special code, which then dictates the subsequent actions and state transitions. This ensures that the state machine operates correctly and maintains proper synchronization and data flow.

Acts as a reset signal to initialize the system to a known state. When active, it sets state and control registers to their initial values, ensuring that the system starts from a defined condition. This signal is crucial for synchronizing the state machine and data path, preventing undefined behavior after power-up or during system reinitialization.

Acts as a data register to store and transfer code bytes in a state machine. When the state machine is in the idle state and valid conditions are met, it loads a code byte from this register and initiates a sequence of data transfers. The value of the code byte determines the next state and the number of subsequent data bytes to be transferred. This register ensures that the correct data is available for processing and helps in maintaining the flow and synchronization of the data stream.

Serves as a data register to store and transfer input data within the state machine. It holds the current byte of data from the input stream and is used to propagate this data to the next stage in the processing pipeline. The value is updated based on the input conditions and the current state, ensuring that valid and relevant data is passed through the system. This register is crucial for maintaining the flow of data and enabling the state machine to process and encode the incoming bytes correctly.

The signal acts as a state flag to manage the different operational modes of the state machine. It dictates the current processing stage, such as waiting for input, handling data segments, or appending final codes. The state flag influences the flow of data and the conditions under which specific actions are taken, like counting non-zero bytes, storing codes, and closing frames. By transitioning between states, it ensures that the system processes input data correctly and generates the appropriate output codes, maintaining the integrity of the data stream.

Serves as a data register to store intermediate state information. It holds the value of the last signal from an internal source, which is used to manage the flow of data to either a temporary buffer or the output interface. This register is updated based on control flags that determine the current operation mode. During a reset, the register is cleared to ensure the system starts in a known state. This mechanism helps in maintaining the integrity and synchronization of the data path.

Acts as a state flag to indicate whether the current frame has failed. This flag is used to propagate error conditions through the state machine. It is updated based on the input data and the current state of the system. When an error is detected, such as a zero or a specific condition in the input data, the flag is set. This flag influences the generation of output codes and the handling of frame termination, ensuring that errors are properly signaled and managed.

Serves as a data register to temporarily store information before it is transferred to the output. This register holds values that are conditionally updated based on control flags, which dictate whether to load new data from an internal source or retain existing temporary data. The register plays a crucial role in synchronizing and managing the flow of data, ensuring that the correct information is available for output. During a reset, this register is cleared, maintaining the integrity and control of the data path.

Acts as a state flag to manage the flow of data from an input to an output, using intermediate storage when necessary. It ensures that data is transferred only when both the source and destination are ready. The state flag is updated based on the readiness of the input and output, and it controls whether data should be moved directly to the output or temporarily stored. During each clock cycle, this flag helps in deciding the data transfer path, ensuring proper synchronization and integrity of the data flow. If a reset condition is detected, the state flag is cleared, resetting the data flow control.

Serves as a data register to temporarily hold user-specific information before it is transferred to the output. This register is updated based on control signals that determine whether to store new data from an internal source or maintain existing data. During each clock cycle, if the appropriate control flag is set, the data in this register is either moved to the output or retained for future use. In case of a reset, the register is cleared to ensure the system starts in a known state, thus supporting the overall data flow management and synchronization.

Acts as a state flag to indicate whether the current frame has failed. This flag is used to propagate error conditions through the state machine. It is set based on specific input conditions, such as receiving an error signal or detecting an invalid data segment. When the frame is closed, this flag determines whether the final code should include an error indication. The flag is reset at the beginning of a new frame and is updated during the processing of each segment.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data segment. It is set when the last byte of a segment is transferred, signaling the completion of the current segment and triggering a transition back to the idle state to fetch the next code byte. This flag ensures that the data flow is properly managed and synchronized, maintaining the correct sequence of operations in the state machine.

Serves as a control signal to manage the flow of data from an input to an output, using intermediate storage when necessary. This signal determines whether the data should be transferred directly to the output or temporarily stored. It ensures that data is only moved when both the source and destination are in the correct state. The control signal is updated based on the readiness of the input and output, and it influences the synchronization and integrity of the data path.

Acts as a data register to store and transfer data bytes in a FIFO buffer. When the state machine is in the segment output state, it retrieves data from this register and sends it to the output. The data register ensures that data is correctly synchronized and transferred, maintaining the flow of information through the system. It is part of the AXI interface, which manages data input and output, and it plays a crucial role in the overall data handling process.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the presence of valid input data. It is used to synchronize and trigger the processing of incoming data within the state machine. When asserted, it enables the state machine to transition between different states, such as idle, segment processing, and final zero appending, based on the current state and the input data. This control signal ensures that data is only processed when it is valid, preventing errors and ensuring proper frame handling.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the validity of data being output from a FIFO buffer. This signal is used to synchronize data transfer, ensuring that the receiving component only processes data when it is valid. In the state machine, this control signal is checked to determine whether to proceed with transferring data and updating the state. When asserted, it enables the propagation of data and associated control signals, facilitating the flow of information through the system.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a frame in the data stream. It is set when the last byte of a segment or frame is processed, and it triggers the closure of the current frame. This flag ensures that the final data and control signals are properly managed, including the appending of additional zero codes if required by the framing mode.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data segment. It is used to signal the completion of a data transfer in both the idle and segment output states. When asserted, it helps to transition the state machine back to the idle state after completing a segment, ensuring that the next code byte is fetched. This flag is crucial for maintaining the correct sequence of data transfers and synchronizing the state transitions in the state machine.

Acts as a data register to store the count of non-zero bytes in a segment. It is used to track the length of segments and determine when to output a code. The value is updated during valid input conditions and is crucial for managing the segment size and triggering the appropriate state transitions. When the segment reaches a specific count, it triggers the output of a code and resets the counter, ensuring that the data is processed and encoded correctly.

Acts as a state flag to manage the count of data bytes to be transferred in a segment. It is loaded with the value of a code byte during the idle state and decremented in the segment output state. This flag controls the transition between states, ensuring that the system remains in the segment output state until all data bytes are transferred. When the count reaches one, it triggers a return to the idle state, allowing the system to prepare for the next segment.

Acts as a state flag to manage the count of non-zero bytes in a segment. It is used to track the number of consecutive non-zero data bytes and is updated during the processing of input data. When a new segment starts, it is initialized, and it increments with each non-zero byte. If the maximum count is reached or a zero byte is encountered, it triggers the storage of the current count and resets or transitions to the next state. This flag ensures that the correct number of bytes is counted and processed, facilitating proper frame encoding and termination.

Acts as a control signal to manage the flow of data from an internal source to either an output or a temporary buffer. When the input is ready and the output is not, this control signal triggers the storage of data in the temporary buffer. It ensures that data is only moved when the output is also ready, preventing data loss or corruption. This signal is part of a larger data transfer control mechanism that synchronizes the movement of data based on the readiness of both the input and output interfaces.

Serves as a data register to store intermediate values in the data path. It holds information that is either transferred from internal storage to the output or temporarily buffered, depending on the current control conditions. This register is crucial for maintaining the integrity of the data flow by ensuring that the correct data is available at the output interface. During reset, it is cleared to prevent any stale data from affecting the system, thus supporting the synchronization and control of the data transfer process.

Serves as a state flag to indicate the end of a data packet in the output stream. It is updated based on the current control conditions, either from an internal source or a temporary buffer. This flag plays a crucial role in signaling the completion of a data transfer, ensuring that the receiving end can properly handle and process the data. During a reset, this flag is cleared, maintaining the correct state and synchronization of the data path.

Acts as a control signal to manage data flow and state transitions in a state machine. It enables the state machine to accept and process input data, transitioning between idle, segment processing, and final zero appending states. When asserted, it allows the state machine to check for valid input data and determine the next actions, such as storing a zero code, initiating a new segment, or closing the frame. This control signal ensures that the state machine operates correctly by synchronizing the input data with the internal state and output codes.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the end of a data segment. It is set when the last byte of a segment is processed, signaling the termination of the current data frame. This flag helps in managing the transition between different states, such as moving from segment processing to frame closure or appending a zero code. It ensures that the data handling logic correctly identifies and processes the end of each data segment, maintaining the integrity of the data flow and frame structure.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the validity of incoming data. It is asserted when valid input data is available and needs to be processed. This signal helps in synchronizing the data transfer, ensuring that the data is only processed when it is valid and ready. It plays a crucial role in managing the flow of data through different states of the state machine, such as idle, segment processing, and final zero appending.

Serves as a state flag to manage the operational mode of a data processing system. It controls the transition between an idle state and a segment output state, directing when to transfer code or data bytes. In the idle state, it waits for valid conditions to initiate a transfer and load a counter. Depending on the code byte, it either remains in the idle state or moves to the segment output state, where it handles data byte transfers and counter decrements. The state flag ensures that the system processes data correctly and synchronizes with external signals, maintaining proper flow and control.

Acts as a control signal to manage the readiness of the output data stream. It is asserted when the downstream component is ready to accept data, enabling the transfer of data from the FIFO. This control signal ensures that data is only transferred when the receiving end can process it, thereby preventing data loss and maintaining synchronization between the FIFO and the downstream logic.

Acts as a control signal to manage the data transfer from internal storage to the output. It is asserted when the input is ready and the output is either ready or currently not valid, enabling the direct transfer of data. If the output is not ready, it triggers the storage of data in a temporary buffer. This control signal ensures that data is only moved when the conditions are appropriate, maintaining the integrity and synchronization of the data flow.

module_description: This module implements a synchronous AXI stream FIFO buffer to manage data flow between input and output interfaces. It synchronizes and temporarily stores data, user-defined signals, and end-of-frame indicators. The FIFO operates based on clock and reset signals, supporting flow control through valid and ready signals. It provides status signals to indicate overflow, bad frame, and good frame conditions, ensuring reliable data transfer and error monitoring.

block_descriptions.description: This code implements a synchronous state machine with reset and enable control. It updates a state register on each clock edge when an input is valid. When a specific control flag is active, it resets the state register to an initial value and clears a validity flag. If the last data in a sequence is detected, it processes the data based on a condition and updates an output register. The validity flag is set to indicate the completion of the processing.

signal_descriptions.description: Acts as a state flag to track the current state of a CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) computation. It updates on each clock cycle when new data is valid. The state flag resets to an initial value upon receiving a reset signal, ensuring the system starts from a known condition. When the last piece of data in a sequence is detected, it processes the final CRC value and updates an output register, setting a validity flag to indicate the completion of the CRC computation.

Serves as a clock signal that synchronizes the state machine operations. On each rising edge, it triggers the update of a state register if the input is valid. It also controls the reset of the state register to an initial value and clears a validity flag when a specific control flag is active. If the last data in a sequence is detected, it processes the data based on a condition and updates an output register, setting a validity flag to indicate the completion of the processing.

Serves as a reset signal to initialize the system. When active, it sets the state register to a predefined initial value and clears a validity flag, effectively resetting the processing state. This control signal ensures that the system starts from a known condition, which is crucial for maintaining correct operation after initialization or when recovering from an error.

Serves as a control signal to initiate the processing of incoming data. When active, it triggers the update of a state register and, if it is the last piece of data in the sequence, it processes the data and updates an output register. The control signal also sets a validity flag to indicate that the processing is complete. This signal ensures that data is only processed when it is valid and ready, maintaining synchronization and integrity in the data flow.

Serves as a state flag to indicate the completion of data processing. It is set when the final data in a sequence is processed and reset during initialization or when a reset condition is met. This flag helps synchronize the output, ensuring that the processed data is valid and ready for further use.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the end of a data sequence. When this signal is active, it triggers the reset of a state register to an initial value and updates an output register based on specific conditions. Additionally, it sets a validity flag to signal the completion of the processing for the current data sequence.

Acts as a data register that stores the final computed value in a cyclic redundancy check (CRC) process. It is updated when the last data of a sequence is detected, and its value is determined based on the current state and input conditions. If a specific condition is met, it processes each bit of the input data and updates the register accordingly. The validity flag is set to indicate that the data in the register is ready for use.

Serves as a state flag to update the internal state of a synchronous process. It is used to transition between states based on the validity and end-of-data conditions. When the last data in a sequence is detected, it influences the computation and storage of a final value in an output register, which is then marked as valid. The state flag is reset to an initial value during a reset condition, ensuring the system starts from a known state.

module_description: This module functions as a synchronous state machine that processes a sequence of data. It updates its internal state on each clock cycle when new data is valid. The state machine resets to an initial state and clears a completion flag when a specific control signal is active. Upon detecting the end of the data sequence, it processes the final data based on a condition and updates an output. A validity flag is then set to signal the completion of the processing.

block_descriptions.description: This code implements a finite state machine (FSM) with two states: idle and payload. In the idle state, it waits for incoming data and initializes the system by setting a reset flag. When data is received, it transitions to the payload state, where it processes the data, updates a cyclic redundancy check (CRC), and sets output signals. If the end of the data is detected, it checks the CRC for errors and resets, then returns to the idle state. The FSM controls the flow of data, manages the CRC, and handles error detection.

This code implements a synchronous state machine with reset and data path control. On the rising edge of the clock, if the reset is active, it initializes the state register to an idle state and resets several control flags and data registers. If the reset is not active, it updates the state register based on the next state, and updates control flags and data registers accordingly. The data path logic includes shifting and updating data validity and data values through multiple stages. Additionally, it handles a CRC (cyclic redundancy check) state, resetting or updating it based on control signals.

This code implements a combinational logic block for data transfer control. It manages the flow of data from an input to an output, using intermediate storage when necessary. The block checks if the input is ready and whether the output can accept new data. If the output is ready or the current output data is not valid, it transfers the input data directly to the output. If the output is not ready, it stores the input data in a temporary location. When the input is not ready but the output is, it moves the data from the temporary storage to the output. This ensures that data is transferred efficiently and without loss.

This code implements a synchronous data path control logic. It updates several registers on the rising edge of the clock. The main functionality includes updating validity and readiness flags, and conditionally storing data, last, and user information from either an intermediate or temporary source to the output registers. The selection between the sources is controlled by two control flags. Additionally, it can store intermediate data into temporary registers based on another control flag. The entire block is reset to initial values when a reset signal is active.

This code block implements a component for generating an 8-bit cyclic redundancy check (CRC) value. It takes an input data stream and a current state, processes the data, and updates the state. The updated state is then used for the next cycle of data processing. This type of logic is typically used in error detection for data transmission.

signal_descriptions.description: Acts as a control signal to update the cyclic redundancy check (CRC) during data processing. It is asserted when valid data is received and the system is in the payload state, ensuring that the CRC is updated with the new data. This control signal is also used to trigger the final CRC comparison at the end of the data stream, which helps in detecting errors. If the CRC does not match the expected value, it signals an error condition.

Acts as a data register to store user information that is part of the data packet. This register is updated during the processing of incoming data and is used to propagate user-specific metadata along with the data. It is set to a default value when the system is idle and is updated with the user information from the input data when the end of the packet is detected. If a cyclic redundancy check (CRC) error is found, it is flagged in this register to indicate an error condition.

Serves as a control signal to manage data flow readiness. It is set to inactive during reset and updated based on the next state's conditions. This signal helps in synchronizing the data transfer by indicating when the system is ready to accept new data, ensuring that the data path operates correctly and efficiently.

Acts as a control signal to manage the readiness for data transfer in the system. It is set based on the current state and input conditions, enabling the flow of data from the input to the output. When asserted, it indicates that the system is ready to accept new data, and it synchronizes with the downstream readiness to ensure proper data propagation. This control signal plays a crucial role in coordinating the data transfer between different stages of the FSM, ensuring that data is processed only when the system is ready.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the end of a data frame. It is set when the last piece of data in the current frame is detected, triggering the system to perform final checks and prepare for the next frame. This signal ensures that the data processing logic correctly identifies the frame boundaries and handles the transition between frames, including error checking and state management.

Serves as a data register to hold intermediate data during the processing of incoming packets. This register is updated with the current input data and is used to pass the data to the output or temporary storage based on control flags. It plays a crucial role in synchronizing and managing the flow of data through the system, ensuring that the correct data is available for further processing or transmission. The register's value is also used in the cyclic redundancy check (CRC) process to validate the integrity of the data.

Serves as a data register to store intermediate state information. It holds the value of the last signal from an intermediate source and updates the output register when specific control flags are asserted. This allows for conditional data flow, enabling the system to selectively pass data from different sources to the output based on the current operational conditions. The register is also reset to its initial state when a reset signal is active, ensuring the system starts in a known condition.

Acts as a control signal to manage the transfer of data from intermediate or temporary storage to the output. It is asserted when the input is not ready but the output is, indicating that data should be moved from the temporary storage to the output. This ensures that data flow is maintained and no data is lost during the transfer process. The control signal synchronizes the data path, ensuring that the correct data, last, and user information are stored in the output registers at the appropriate time.

Serves as a data register in the data path, storing intermediate data values as they are shifted through multiple stages. This register is part of a pipeline that processes incoming data, ensuring that each data segment is correctly aligned and available for subsequent operations, such as CRC calculation and error detection. The register updates its value on the rising edge of the clock, and its content is used to verify the integrity of the data by comparing it with the calculated CRC.

Acts as a control signal to manage data flow and state transitions in the finite state machine. It is used to determine when valid data is available for processing and influences the transition between idle and payload states. When asserted, it enables the propagation of data through the system and triggers the update of the cyclic redundancy check (CRC). This control signal also plays a role in error detection by validating the CRC at the end of the data transfer.

Serves as a data register to store the last indicator in a data path. It is updated with the last value from either an intermediate or temporary source, depending on the control flags. This register helps in maintaining the integrity of the data stream by correctly marking the end of a packet. The update occurs synchronously with the clock, and the register is reset to an initial state when a reset signal is active.

Serves as a data register in a multi-stage shift register used to propagate data validity. It captures the validity of incoming data and shifts it through successive stages on each clock cycle, ensuring that the data is synchronized and properly aligned for further processing. This register is part of a larger data path that also includes other registers for data values, all of which are updated in a coordinated manner to maintain data integrity and timing.

Acts as a control signal to manage the flow of data from an input to an output. It is asserted when the input data is valid and the output is ready to accept new data, enabling the transfer. If the output is not ready, it stores the input data in a temporary location. When the input is not ready but the output is, it moves the data from the temporary storage to the output. This ensures that data is transferred efficiently and without loss.

Acts as a control signal to manage the validity of data transfer between input and output. It ensures that data is transferred only when the output is ready or the current output data is not valid. If the output is not ready, it stores the input data in a temporary location. When the input is not ready but the output is, it transfers the data from the temporary storage to the output. This control signal helps in maintaining efficient and lossless data flow by coordinating the transfer and storage of data.

Serves as a data register to temporarily hold information that can be selectively transferred to the output. This register is part of a synchronous data path control logic, where it stores intermediate data. The data in this register is conditionally loaded into the output based on specific control flags. It helps in managing the flow and storage of data, ensuring that the correct information is available for output when needed. During a reset, the register is cleared to its initial state.

Acts as a control signal to indicate the presence of user-defined information in the data stream. It is used to propagate additional metadata alongside the main data payload. This signal is checked and passed through to the output when the end of the data packet is detected, and it can also trigger error handling if the CRC check fails. The signal ensures that any user-specific flags or information are correctly transferred and processed.

Acts as a state flag to indicate the presence of a CRC error. It is set when the computed CRC does not match the expected value at the end of the data frame, signaling an error condition. This flag is used to update the error status in the system, which can then be used for further processing or error handling. The flag is reset at the beginning of each new frame and is only asserted if a mismatch is detected, ensuring that the system can identify and respond to corrupted data frames.

Acts as a state flag to store the next value of the cyclic redundancy check (CRC). It is used to update the CRC state during data processing. When new data is received, this flag is updated with the new CRC value, which is then used in subsequent cycles for error detection. If the end of the data is detected, the current CRC value is compared against the expected value to check for errors. This flag is crucial for maintaining the integrity of the data being processed and ensuring that any discrepancies are identified.

Acts as a control signal to reset the data path and state registers. It is asserted at the end of data processing, specifically when the last data segment is detected. This signal ensures that the system returns to an initial state, preparing it for the next data transfer.
